name: EV Charging Infrastructure Attack
description: Compromise an EV charging network via OCPP protocol abuse, backend cloud exploitation, or charger firmware attacks to disrupt charging, steal payment data, or destabilize the local distribution grid.
root_objective: Compromise EV charging infrastructure to disrupt charging services or destabilize the grid

nodes.title: Disrupt EV Charging Network or Destabilize Grid via Chargers
nodes.description: Attack an EV charging point operator (CPO) to disable charging stations at scale, exfiltrate payment card data, or coordinate rapid load swings that stress the local distribution grid.

nodes.title: Compromise OCPP Backend Management System
nodes.description: Attack the cloud-hosted Central System (CSMS) that manages all chargers via OCPP (Open Charge Point Protocol) to issue mass commands.

nodes.title: Attack Individual Charger Hardware/Firmware
nodes.description: Compromise individual charging stations at the firmware or hardware level to install persistent backdoors or modify charging behaviour.

nodes.title: Exploit OCPP WebSocket Endpoint
nodes.description: Connect to the CSMS OCPP WebSocket endpoint with a spoofed charger identity. Many OCPP 1.6J implementations use basic auth or charger serial as the sole identity.

nodes.title: API Takeover of Fleet Management Portal
nodes.description: Exploit IDOR or broken access control in the CPO's fleet management REST API to gain administrative control over all charge points.

nodes.title: Issue Mass RemoteStop / Reset Commands
nodes.description: Use OCPP RemoteStopTransaction or Reset commands to simultaneously disable hundreds of chargers during peak demand.

nodes.title: Physical Access to Charger Debug Port
nodes.description: Open the charger cabinet to access UART/JTAG debug ports on the controller board. Extract firmware, implant backdoor, or dump credentials.

nodes.title: Exploit Charger WiFi/Bluetooth for Local Access
nodes.description: Many chargers expose WiFi AP or BLE for installer configuration with default credentials, providing a wireless entry point to the charger's OS.

nodes.title: Intercept Payment Card Data via Skimmer
nodes.description: Install a contactless payment skimmer overlay on the charger's NFC/RFID reader to capture payment card credentials from EV owners.

nodes.title: OCPP 1.6 Weak Charger Authentication
nodes.description: OCPP 1.6J relies on charger serial number and optional basic auth with no mutual TLS, making charger identity spoofing trivial.

nodes.title: Physical Access to Unmonitored Charging Station
nodes.description: Attacker needs brief unsupervised physical access to install the payment skimmer hardware on the charger's card reader.

nodes.title: Charger WiFi Bridges Local Network to Backend
nodes.description: The charger's internal WiFi and LTE interfaces bridge local wireless access to the OCPP backend connection, enabling pivot to the CSMS.
